data.title: Trump Set To Break WTO Rules With Potential Pharma Tariffs
data.description:
By Diederik Stadig

President Trump's announcement marks a clear shift in trade policy

The tariff threats keep on coming from the US President. On Tuesday, along with cars and semiconductors, he stated that his administration intends to hit pharmaceuticals with 25%+ tariffs. This marks a clear break from WTO rules, which exempt all pharmaceutical products from tariffs.

On the timing, Trump stated that the tariffs for cars would come into effect on 2 April, but we're still not sure if and when pharma may be hit. He wants to allow producers to expand their manufacturing capacity in the US. However, this is complex and unlikely to happen overnight.

European producers would be most affected by the tariff

We estimate that the US consumed roughly $560 billion of pharmaceutical products in 2024, of which roughly $200 billion was imported. In other words, the US imports roughly 36% of its pharmaceuticals. In recent years, Ireland has been the main import partner of the US, followed by Germany and Switzerland, and these are the countries that are going to be hit particularly hard should the threat become a reality.

These countries also export a mix of branded and generic pharmaceuticals. Branded pharmaceuticals are more expensive and make up a larger share of import value, yet 91% of all prescription drugs are generic, which means reliance on the availability of generic drugs is far greater than reliance on branded pharma (FDA). This is in contrast to countries such as India and China (responsible for roughly 6% and 2% of US imports, respectively) that mostly export generic pharmaceuticals to the US. Their importance to US consumers is, therefore, understated in the figure below.

Ireland, Germany, Switzerland top exporters of pharmaceuticals to the US

US imports of pharmaceutical products by country share, 2023

Increasing manufacturing capacity will take time

Trump's demand for more US manufacturing capacity in the pharmaceutical sector is warranted. In 2021, a report from the Biden administration found that that capacity restriction was leading to drug shortages. However, according to the ASPR, several US manufacturing plants have been closed over recent years as a result of lower operating and labour costs offshore, price pressure and dependencies on other countries for raw materials. These factors complicate a swift expansion of manufacturing capacity.

That said, the capacity utilisation rate of manufacturing in the US is around 80%, according to the Federal Reserve, and this figure is lower for pharmaceuticals. Indeed, for pharma, these rates are kept relatively low to allow for flexibility when demand for a specific drug surges. Still, idle capacity in the US could be utilised to produce more pharmaceuticals on US soil. However, to utilise idle capacity, producers need to possess the raw materials required, which isn't always easy.

Moreover, producers of generic pharmaceuticals in India and China are highly specialised and enjoy significant economies of scale. This makes it difficult for US producers of generic pharmaceuticals with thin margins to compete, but this advantage could shrink as tariffs increase. In addition, repurposing currently idle capacity will take time: a report by Washington University found that we're looking at something between two and three years. This is likely far longer than Trump would like.

Costs for consumers and non-American producers likely to increase

Although the timing of tariffs is uncertain, we believe that it is likely that Trump will not want to wait two to three years to impose a tariff on pharmaceuticals. If tariffs were to be imposed, this would likely increase the costs of prescription drugs in the US, hurting consumers. This is especially troublesome for generic drugs with thin margins that are unlikely to be reshored to the US; dependency on these drugs is large and, with no viable production alternative, prices for consumers will simply increase.

Moreover, it would increase costs for producers that would either have to navigate tariffs or increase production capacity in the US, both of which are costly to do. Speaking on this, Takeda CEO Christophe Weber stated: "At Takeda (TAK), we have a manufacturing network focused on US, Europe, Singapore and Japan. It's a global network, which was built on the premise of free trade... If there are more forces against free trade, we'll have to adapt over time."

Lastly, if other blocs institute tariffs in response, this would further drive up costs and pharmaceutical supply chain disruption. This is not good for anyone. Especially given that populations are ageing and demand for medicines is increasing, tariffs would put unnecessary stress on medicine prices and the affordability of healthcare globally.

So, although the demand for more US manufacturing capacity in the pharmaceutical sector is warranted, tariffs are likely the wrong way to go about achieving this.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Roku: Why I Am Aggressively Buying At 1-Year Highs
data.description:
Streaming company Roku (NASDAQ:ROKU) achieved, for the first time in the company’s history, platform revenue exceeding $1.0B in the fourth fiscal quarter, showing 25% year-over-year growth. The streaming platform also exceeded bottom and top line estimates and saw a drastically improved profitability profile in FY 2024, in terms of both operating income and adjusted EBITDA. Roku's results and guidance were well-received by investors, which pushed shares 14% higher after earnings, to new 1-year highs.

Most importantly, Roku returned to positive average revenue per user growth in Q4'24 which indicates that the company’s platform is improving its customer monetization. The outlook for FY 2025 is positive and with the streaming firm set to lower its losses this year, I see potential for an upside re-pricing of Roku’s shares.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Roku a strong buy in my last work -- Inflection Point -- and recommended investors to buy the dip in the price of the streaming firm’s shares in early January 2025. While Roku is still losing money on its operations, the streaming company guided for positive operating income in FY 2026 which could support a revaluation of the company’s shares. Favorable long-term streaming trends (the adoption of streaming services as a substitute of cable TV) also benefit companies like Roku, and the platform returned to positive ARPU growth in the fourth-quarter.

Better-than-expected Q4'24

Roku easily beat analysts' estimates for the fourth-quarter, surpassing the bottom line estimate by $0.19 per-share and the top line average prediction by $52.2M. The streaming company was helped by strong platform growth throughout the fourth-quarter, as well as improving customer monetization.

Active account growth, customer monetization and improving profitability profile

In the fourth fiscal quarter, Roku's active accounts surged 12% year-over-year to a record of 89.8M due to strong growth in the holiday quarter that also led to an upsurge in streaming hours (+18% Y/Y). Streaming hours on the popular Roku Channel increased a massive 82% Y/Y and with streaming platforms continuing to boom, Roku is set for continual top line and subscriber growth in the quarters ahead.

At the end of FY 2024, the company boasted almost 90M accounts on its platform. Importantly, holiday-related tailwinds led to an improvement in the average revenue per user/ARPU figure as well, which gained 4% to $41.49. In the previous quarter, Roku’s ARPU was essentially flat, highlighting monetization challenges for the company on the Roku-branded streaming platform.

Actual Results

Q4'23

Q1'24

Q2'24

Q3'24

Q4'24

Growth Y/Y

Active Accounts (millions)

80.0

81.6

83.6

12%

Streaming Hours (billions)

29.1

Average Revenue Per User/ARPU ($)

$39.92

$40.65

$40.68

$41.10

$41.49

4%

(Source: Author)

In the fourth-quarter, Roku continued to lose money on its streaming operations, unfortunately. However, the streaming platform has been able to successively lower its losses, which indicates an overall improving profitability profile. In the fourth-quarter, Roku generated an operating loss of $39.1M, showing an improvement of 62% year-over-year. Roku's solid subscriber and ARPU growth trends have resulted in a strong outlook for the company's FY 2026: Roku expects to become operating income-profitable next year, which could push shares into a new up-leg, in my opinion.

Roku is also seeing a profound improvement in its adjusted EBITDA profile due to growing accounts and improving monetization, in addition to past cost cuts in a bid to improve platform efficiencies. In FY 2024, Roku achieved positive adjusted EBITDA of $260M, representing a 65X factor increase over the comparable period a year earlier.

Roku’s outlook for FY 2025

For the first fiscal quarter of FY 2025, the streaming company expects to deliver $4.6B in revenue, which implies 12% year-over-year growth. EBITDA is expected at $350M, implying 35% Y/Y growth. Key levers for Roku's growth will be ad revenues, subscribers and ARPU, for which the streaming platform does not give any guidance. However, if the streaming company can continue to grow its ARPU specifically, I would not be surprised to see Roku outperform its revenue target for FY 2025.

Roku’s valuation

Roku’s shares are valued at a price-to-revenue ratio of 2.7X which compares against a P/S ratio, also on a forward FY 2026 basis, of 8.9X for Netflix (NFLX)... which is arguably Roku’s most serious streaming rival.

Walt Disney (DIS), which is not fully comparable due to the presence of non-streaming businesses (like entertainment parks), is priced at a forward P/S ratio of 1.98X. I like Disney because the streaming platform has managed to turn its direct-to-consumer business around in the last two years -- mirroring fundamental improvements for Roku in terms of adjusted EBITDA since FY 2023. As Disney improves its profitability profile and returns more cash to shareholders, I see significant upside potential for the entertainment and streaming company.

I believe Roku, as I explained in my last article on the streaming platform, could be valued at a price-to-revenue ratio of 4.0X which implies ~48% revaluation potential and a fair value of ~$140 per-share. This fair value price target could be realized by the end of next year, in my opinion, under the condition that the streaming company continues to narrow its operating losses and expands on its recent ARPU gains.

Risks with Roku

While Roku experiences a positive uptrend in its average revenue per user figure, highlighting improving customer monetization, seasonal holiday effects have provided a boost here. In my past work on the streaming platform, I repeatedly said that a revaluation can only happen if Roku improves customer monetization and narrows its losses. If Roku fails to consistently expand its subscriber base and grow its ARPU, shares will face revaluation headwinds and may disappoint growth investors going forward.

Closing thoughts

Roku submitted a solid earnings scorecard for the fourth fiscal quarter last week, and the company beat expectations on both the bottom and the top line. Roku's Q4'24 results were well-received, with shares gaining 14% and hitting a new 1-year high... and I am still adding here.

The outlook for FY 2025 is positive, and the streaming platform expects to achieve double-digit top line growth in FY 2025 as well as positive operating income in FY 2026... both of which may help Roku achieve a higher valuation. The improving profitability picture, based off of operating income as well as EBITDA, is the real story for the streaming firm and if Roku can execute here, I can see shares grow into a higher valuation.

Shares of Roku remain fundamentally undervalued and, as opposed to Netflix, are much more affordable and therefore also have a much more favorable risk profile, in my opinion.

data.title: Intel Stock: Elemental Analysis Of An Acquisition (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
It's now or never

In my last article on Intel (NASDAQ:INTC), I argued the following:

The gigantic strain of building out the US semiconductor-FAB network was expending a lot more on CAPEX than cash from operations was bringing in, resulting in ongoing negative free cash flow that remains.

There's really only one thesis that works with this stock in my opinion, it's a play on the "cheapness" of assets on a historical basis combined with the optimism that a new CEO will be a better marshal of resources than previous CEO Pat Gelsinger.

Now I'm not so sure that the company will need to marshal resources at all. Maybe they reserve as much capital as possible and simply make the company as attractive as possible for a suitor that is in a better financial situation than the beleaguered Intel.

Why I say it's now or never is that we seem to be approaching a "no holds barred" era of possible deal making with the Trump 2.0 administration seemingly putting success and GDP growth above all else. If there was ever a 4-year period in US history to merge some big semiconductor companies together, or buy significant pieces of one, this would be it.

Intel's "capital stack"

First off, before we look at the possible new deal propositions that have been making the airwaves, let's look at Intel's capital stack. The company market cap is now down to $102.19 Billion USD versus $50.71 Billion in total debt, much of which is due to former CEO Pat Gelsinger's decision to pivot into a FAB-first model trying to be the Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) of America.

The long-term debt situation might turn Intel into the Taiwan Semiconductor of America after all, the only caveat is it might be run by Taiwan Semiconductor.

The contenders and their propositions

Fresh off the press from Seeking Alpha:

Intel led chips stocks higher on Tuesday after reports that Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and Broadcom (AVGO) could each look to acquire parts of the company.

Although the various elements of selling the company off in a couple of parts are attractive, we also have to analyze just how likely these companies could be in pulling this acquisition off from a financial point of view and what a company like Intel might be valued at now that their EBITDA is dried up at the moment. We can really only look at assets and what the acquiring companies might be able to do with them.

Firstly, what exactly is the correct comp set for how many times book you pay for a semiconductor company:

STOCK PRICE TO BOOK
Intel :INTC 1.03
Micron :(MU) 2.37
Marvell :(MRVL) 6.89
NXP Semiconductors :(NXPI) 6.21
Qualcomm :(QCOM) 7.08
AMD: (AMD) 3.19
AVERAGE 4.46

Within this data set of comps they all have different levels of businesses split between FAB and design mixes, we can assume Intel would be at the upper end of this spectrum. We can see from the first chart that Intel at one time traded for around 4 X book value in the early 2000s and near that level pre-covid era.

With an average market price paid to book value of 4.46 X for this group currently, even dropping Intel to 3-4 X [still probably high but there is usually a premium paid in an acquisition], the stock would be very undervalued compared to the current FAB network the company has built out.

If the stock ended up trading for 3 X book value, that would be a market cap of $306 Billion USD. Add in debt of $50.7 Billion and back out cash of $22.06 Billion and you would have an enterprise value of $334.64 Billion to take the company whole in my opinion.

It will probably be the case that only a portion of the business would end up being acquired and there would be a remainder, [remain-co] so Enterprise Value is not relevant. Still, the FAB and design units could be worth as much as 50% of Intel's overall value, maybe more. I believe at least $150-$200 Billion would be needed to takeover the FAB and design businesses.

Intel highlighted the massive spending spree on new FABs in a recent media piece, below are some highlights:

  • Arizona: Intel is investing more than $32 billion to build two new leading-edge chip factories
  • Oregon: Intel plans to invest more than $36 billion in its R&D operations in Hillsboro, Oregon.
  • Ohio: 2 plants at a cost of over $28 billion.
  • New Mexico: $4 Billion in investments.

Totaling $100 Billion in new investments, this makes up more than half of the gross PPE line item at current [although not all of these assets are included in gross PPE since they are not completed]. We can observe the growth in gross PPE [basically real estate, factories, and machines] below:

A gross asset analysis of Intel

A massive near doubling of gross PPE from 2020 to the present [again including the depreciation expense being added back], representing the cost of new construction plus old remaining operations. But, and a big but this is! A company builds assets to create cash flow, and at the same time cash flow, using EBITDA as a proxy has tanked. Again a lot of this new infrastructure has not yet been given a chance to prove its worthiness, but unfortunately, the product Intel produces itself has had brand destruction ongoing during this buildout process.

That's not going to help once you open up your new facilities and claim to now finally be able to build the latest and greatest chips and GPUs.

The necessary elements of an acquisition

So, now we have the playing field. Lots of assets and new assets that are still not even yet completed, with abundant tax-advantaged depreciation expense in the tank. Are the elements there for sale?

  • Target has excessive debt levels
  • Target has valuable assets
  • Target has high potential cash flows when debt is restructured
  • Target has a brand and relationships
  • Buyers have ample cash or have further borrowing capacity

The last two points of what I would consider necessary elements to motivate a sale and acquisition amongst parties are a bit more questionable. The brand name of Intel is not great at the moment and would probably make more sense to just rebrand everything Broadcom if they become the chip designer and marketer, while Taiwan Semiconductor would become the fabricator. This type of partnership would make a lot of sense.

As for the financing capacity of the two, that also takes some further investigation.

Cash and leverage capabilities of the buyers

Broadcom

Broadcom is already pushing up against 3 X EBITDA for their long-term debt situation at 2.82 X. Cash and short-term investments are even lower than Intel at $9.34 Billion. Broadcom would probably need some private financing, possibly from Taiwan Semiconductor themselves, in order to buy the design business.

Taiwan Semiconductor

Taiwan Semiconductor is under-leveraged with only a .48 debt-to-EBITDA ratio. Typically credit rating agencies consider 0-3 X EBITDA healthy. Above 3 X could harm institutional grade credit ratings. Taiwan semiconductor is the key to this deal with $73.65 Billion in cash and short-term investments and a possible borrowing capacity of $180 Billion give or take [basically another $150 Billion in additional leverage capacity.]

I don't think the group would have the firepower to buy Intel whole if they wanted to. There will be some remaining pieces of Intel after the design and FAB businesses are sold [if they are sold].

Here are the revenue-generating divisions for Intel and what they were doing last quarter:

REVENUE SOURCE AMOUNT IN MILLIONS OF USD PERCENT OF REVENUE YOY GROWTH/SHRINK
OPERATING SEGMENT [DESKTOP, NOTEBOOK CHIPS, DATA CENTER, EDGE COMPUTING AND AI] 12190 69.33% -1.6%
FOUNDRY 4352 24.7% -8%
ALTERA 412 2.3% -49%
MOBILEYE 485 2.7% -54%
OTHER 142

.8%

 -21%
GROSS REVENUES 17581

As we can see, the operating segment is still the largest revenue generator but would not be as valuable as the shiny new vacuum-sealed assets in the foundry business that have yet to have the wrapper taken off in many cases.

Altera is set to be spun off. Mobileye is still a controlling stake for Intel and several portions of the company's operating segment will most likely remain. This would be interesting to see, what would Intel do with the hypothetical cash proceeds? Would investors get shares of Broadcom and Taiwan Semiconductor in a part cash part stock deal? Anything would be welcome at this point, in my opinion.

Risks and summary

I am upgrading this to a strong buy. I believe this is just the beginning of a trend we will see where companies will start getting more eager to jump on M&A while they assume there are more favorable regulators sitting behind their desks. This particular purchase of FABs by Taiwan Semiconductor is also a hedge against Taiwan itself one day being absorbed into mainland China. They are the most valuable asset Taiwan has, and this quick diversification into the United States secures a US National Security interest as well.

The risks are that the Trump regulators squash talks of taking over a long-standing national blue chip [at least most of it] and refuse to accept US inferiority on this one manufacturing item [where the US is clearly inferior to Taiwan's capabilities]. He may further choose to subsidize Intel and lead them down an even bigger debt hole that ends up making an inferior product for those in need of the latest and greatest chip production.

I like that this M&A chatter for Intel has begun. For as long as it remains without being shot down, it should drive Intel higher.

data.title: Bank of Korea Preview: Rate Cuts Resume, But Inflation Caution Clouds Outlook
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang

The BoK is expected to resume its policy easing, but the decision may not be unanimous

We believe that the Bank of Korea's priority is supporting weak domestic demand, even as concerns about the inflation outlook keep policymakers on a hawkish footing.

The USDKRW fell to 1,440, but the move is in line with DXY trends. The political turmoil in Seoul that triggered excessive KRW weakness has abated since January's policy meeting. Consumer-price inflation is rising at a 2% rate, and pipeline prices are on the rise. Even so, we expect inflation to remain within the BoK's target range this year, giving it more latitude to cut. For now, the manufacturing sector is holding up relatively well despite tariff threats. US President Trump's reciprocal tariffs are likely to hit Korean manufacturers, increasing the odds the BoK's will remain skewed toward monetary easing on 25 February.

Yet, caution abounds. The BoK worries rate cuts could accelerate the rise in domestic household debt and property prices. As such, the BoK is likely to be extremely cautious in telegraphing more rate cuts. The central bank also is keeping a close watch on how the widening gap between the US and Korea yields affects the KRW.

Overall, we expect the BoK to cut rates four times this year, though each decision will likely be a close call. Case in point: next week's policy meeting could see a dissenting vote. Fewer than three BoK board members have expressed a clear openness to ease, which clouds market expectations over the next three months.

Next week, the BoK will publish its quarterly macro outlook report. We expect the BoK will lower its GDP forecast for 2025 to the 1.5%-1.6% range, which is still higher than ING's forecast of 1.3% growth, while hiking its inflation forecast to 2.0%.

Consumer sentiment continued to recover for a second month after a sharp drop in December

The worst fears about December's political turbulence slamming consumer confidence haven't been realized. Since the end of 2024, data show that while sentiment remains below neutral, it hasn't plunged as feared. Nor has financial volatility surged.

Meanwhile, inflation expectations eased to 2.7% in December from 2.8% in the previous month, despite the recent acceleration in inflation. Expectations for inflation are relatively tame, which may come as a relief to the BoK. But with Trump's tariffs heading Korea's way, the BoK will take a wait-and-see approach to adding more monetary stimulus.

Consumer sentiment improved for a second straight month

Pipeline inflation is on the rise

Producer prices held steady at 1.7% year-on-year in January for the second month, but monthly growth (0.6% MoM, not-seasonally adjusted) has accelerated in the past three months. Fresh food (4.0%) and energy prices (4.0%) rose the most. Services prices (0.4%), such as eating out (0.3%) and transportation (0.3%), also contributed. We believe that the rise in pipeline inflation will likely feed into consumer inflation with a time lag.

We expect consumer prices to hover around 2% for some time, but risks are to the upside. As a result, the BoK will be cautious about cutting interest rates. If prices rise more than expected, the government will try to curb inflation with various measures.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: AIYY: C3.ai's Business Is Too Speculative For An Income Strategy
data.description:
Overview

YieldMax AI Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:AIYY) is an option ETF that aims to track the underlying price movements of C3.ai, Inc (AI). AIYY launched back in November of 2023 and has been one of the worst performing YieldMax funds to ever release. As a result, AIYY has a much smaller total of assets under management, which currently sits around $110M. The poor performance can be attributed to both the lackluster performance of common shares of AI, as well as the option strategy that AI deploys that limits the upside price appreciation that can be experienced.

The main appeal of a fund like AIYY is that it currently offers a massive dividend yield of about 68%, according to the latest fund overview. Investors should be aware that the distribution amounts change month to month, so if you are reading this months after release, there is a high probability that the dividend yield has shifted. Looking at the performance since inception, we can see that AIYY's price has declined by 64.7%. Even when including all of the high distributions that were paid, the total return still amounts to negative 22.8%. Other YieldMax funds have experienced a similar price decline but AIYY is one of the rare exceptions where the distributions have not been able to offset.

C3.ai's price has been highly volatile over the last year, suffering from a continuous decline in the first half of 2024 before finally seeing a positive price movement. The option strategy that AIYY implements limits the capital appreciation that can be experienced, so I anticipate AIYY always underperforming from a price perspective. AIYY should only be utilized as an income vehicle to accompany an already existing position within C3.ai. This is because AIYY's emphasis income can allow investors to benefit from both mild downward market pressure and a sideways moving price. Let's first start by taking a look at the fund's strategy and how it generates the high amount of income for shareholders.

Fund Strategy & Associated Risks

AIYY is advised by Tidal Investments and sports a very reasonable management fee of 0.99%, considering that investors are receiving a large dividend yield in return. AIYY implements a synthetic option strategy to generate the income needed to fuel the large distributions. The term 'synthetic' refers to the fact that AIYY doesn't actually hold any underlying common shares of C3.ai. This synthetic approach can be riskier as it may increase the overall volatility experienced. So how does it replicate the daily price movements of C3.ai without owning shares?

Well, it does so by simultaneously buying call options and selling put options, which simulates a similar price movement as C3.ai. If we look at the price change of the two assets, we can see that they share a similar price movement over the short term. An issue arises when we take this sort of synthetic approach and apply it over a longer time horizon to an underperforming stock.

It all can get very complicated if you don't have a basic understanding of how options work. I like to read through each fund's prospectus because it lays out the different strategies that are implemented. In the table below, we can see that AIYY has the ability to implement both ATM (at-the-money) and OTM (out-of-the-money) options to get the desired results. There's a bit of a balancing act that needs to happen here because each strategy comes with its own set of benefits and tradeoffs.

For instance, ATM options means that AIYY will select a strike price that is very close to the underlying market price of the asset it is trying to replicate. For reference, common shares of C3.ai trade around the $32 per share. With an ATM approach, AIYY may select a strike price of $33 per share, which allows for limited upside, but may grant a higher option premium. Conversely, an OTM approach may see AIYY selecting a strike price at $37 per share, which allows for a bit more price upside to be experienced, but lower option premiums are received.

Remember how I said the issue with this strategy becomes more obvious over longer periods of time? Well, if we take a look at the price change comparison between AIYY and C3.ai, we can see that AIYY is down nearly 65% since inception while C3.ai is up 6.5%. This makes it extremely unlikely for option ETFs to recover from large downside movements. Therefore, the main risk would be continued downside deterioration that may lead to AIYY going through a reverse split.

We can also see that AIYY fails to partake in the upside swings that C3 has experienced, even in late 2024 when C3.ai's price went from $25 per share up above $42 per share. While the upside is limited, the downside is not. I would normally be more open to this kind of risk when the underlying stock that YieldMax is trying to track is a company that is more of an established player.

For instance, I previously covered YieldMax MSFT Option Income Strategy ETF (MSFO) which aims to provide a high distribution while tracking the daily price movements of Microsoft (MSFT). MSFT is a huge player in the technology sector with many well-established revenue streams. The stock generally experiences less volatility on a daily basis, which helps limit the overall price decline. As a result, the price decline that MSFO experiences is a lot less dramatic in comparison, even though MSFT's price has been on a decline over the last three months. The main issue that I have with AIYY is that the underlying company it tracks is a speculative play and lacks the same level of well-established revenue streams.

C3 AI Uncertainty

C3.ai publicly launched in December 2020 and has seen its share price decline by more than 65% since then. Just for some quick context, C3.ai is an enterprise artificial intelligence company that builds different AI-based software for businesses. They implement different AI software that can help companies improve their efficiency related to capital management, decision-making, and general analytics. C3.ai is still in the growth phase of their business and has been making efforts to expand on the amount of bookings that they acquire. However, there is so much competition in the AI space and investors have seemed to favor Palantir (PLTR) and Snowflake (SNOW) who both offer very similar services.

Looking at the factor grades for C3.ai, we have a pretty mixed bag. On one front, valuation looks attractive and the stock earns a B+ rating following the price decline since inception. However, profitability metrics earn a D+, which can be attributed to the company's poor margins in comparison to the rest of the sector. Just to ensure that we have the most updated figures, I will be referring to the most recently released Q2 earnings report.

Most notably, AI reported a negative EPS of $0.06 per share, which beat estimates by $0.10. Similarly, revenue for the quarter amounted to $94M, which beat estimates by $3M and represents a year over year growth of 28.8%. The obvious issue here is that the company is still not profitable. The business will be releasing its Q3 earnings in about a week, but I don't expect anything significant to change.

C3.ai released their FY25 guidance and management estimates total revenue to fall between the range of $378M-$398M. This is going to be driven by an increase in subscription revenue as the business increases its efforts to pilots and trials that can help increase overall exposure to new opportunities. Despite this representing a potential year over year growth between 22% to 28%, Non-GAAP losses from operations are still estimated to be above $105M.

The issue with the outlook for losses is that C3 is not a startup anymore and has not yet seen a profitable year. For instance, free cash flow sits at negative $0.47 per share. Looking at the table below, we can see that free cash flow amounts to negative $39.5M. While this is a slight improve from the prior year's negative free cash flow of $55.1M, it is ultimately still losing capital. Therefore, I consider AI to be a speculative play here and this is the main thing keeping me cautious about initiating a position.

Just to give you an idea of what their current bookings by industry look like, we can refer to the chart below. We can see that Manufacturing accounts for the largest slice of their bookings at 35.7%. This is followed by bookings in Federal Defense Aerospace and High Technology companies, accounting for 33.3% and 16.1%, respectively. While the company's effort in growing its pilot and trials profile can yield larger contracts in the future, I prefer to stay away from this speculative stock. I just don't see anything that yet warrants a change in direction for C3.ai.

Dividend Utilization

As of the latest declared monthly dividend of $0.371 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 68%. The dividend yield changes every time a new distribution amount is announced, so this yield is not set in stone. Looking over the dividend history, we can see highly variable payout amounts since inception. The payout amounts are determined by how profitable the underlying option strategy was during a given month, implied volatility, date until expiration, and other factors.

The most ideal scenario for AIYY would be for the underlying price of AI to remain stable from this point forward. This would allow the distributions to eventually take the total return into the positive territory. Since the distribution yield sits around 68%, investors would be able to technically collect their initial investment back in less than two years. $10,000 invested at the current price would assumably generate you $6,800 in annual dividend income. After collecting dividends greater than your initial investment amount of $10,000, you'd have effectively reached what I consider to be 'house money status'.

However, I am not suggesting this as the specific use-case that readers should implement. I only want to demonstrate how a high distribution like this can be utilized when held over a longer period of time. Investors should also consider that these high distributions have the potential to produce unfavorable tax results. The latest section19(a) notice released from YieldMax only dates back to October, so it may be outdated, but I wanted to use it as a point of reference. We can see that 37% of the distributions were funded by net investment income, while the remaining 63% was funded by return of capital.

Distributions funded by net investment income are typically classified as ordinary dividends. Ordinary dividends have the most unfavorable tax consequences when held outside of a tax advantaged account. Return of capital distributions are a bit more tax friendly since the income itself isn't taxed. Instead, an investor's cost basis is reduced, and the taxes are deferred until the time of sale. If taxes are a big factor for you, I would consult with a tax professional before considering a position in AIYY.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am rating AIYY as a hold for now because of the speculative nature of the underlying business that the fund aims to track. C3.ai hasn't been able to produce a positive free cash flow for investors and despite growing revenues, I do not believe the business offers the most ideal levels of performance consistency for an income approach to be suitable. If C3.ai's price continues to decline, AIYY can be in threat of a reverse split. Since assets under management are comparatively small to other YieldMax funds, AIYY could also face the risk of a liquidation if the appeal of the fund fails to obtain more capital. The option strategy that AIYY implements caps the upside potential that can be experienced, but it doesn't look like C3 is aligned to provide any upside as long as the business continues to see net losses.

data.title: SHLD: Record Defense Spending Can Accelerate Returns In 2025
data.description:
As corporations revel they have been experiencing unprecedented demand with defense budget’s across the world approaching new highs, it appears to be one of the ideal time to invest in defense sector through investment vehicles like Global X Defense Tech ETF (NYSEARCA:SHLD). After the record-breaking financial numbers from defense companies in the last year, a forecast of double-digit earnings growth in 2025 is expected to accelerate investors returns. In addition, the ETF’s attractive PE ratio with the robust earnings growth power offers an entry point. Therefore, I initiate coverage of SHLD with a buy rating.

Record Defense Spending is Expected in 2025

The defense market saw one of the fastest growth rates in 2025 compared to the previous years due to multiple reasons, including geopolitical tensions and a race to achieve strategic superiority with the latest technologies and equipment. In 2024, Russian aggression against Ukraine, Europe and United States contributed significantly in lifting global defense budget to 1.90% of GDP from 1.8% in 2023 and 1.6% in 2022, according to a report from International Institute for Strategic Studies. Europe lifted its defense spending by 11.7% to reach its goal of spending 2% of GDP in defense, while the President Trump seeks them to lift it to 5%.

Moreover, after increasing defense budget to 6.7% of GDP, Russia plans to spend 7.5% its GDP in defense in 2024. Whereas, defence budgets continued to grow at a moderate pace in Asia. China is expected to increase its defense spending by mid-single-digit percentage in 2025 while Japan plans to grow its defense spending by 9.4% in 2025 to $4.8 billion.

Defense spending in the MENA region accelerated in 2024 due to Israeli-Gaza conflict. Algeria, Saudi Arabia, UAE and other Middle Eastern countries have lifted their defense spending to new record levels. In 2025, Algeria's defense spending is projected to reach $25.1 billion, up 16% from 2024. Saudi Arabia has allocated $78 billion for defense in 2025 compared to $75.8 billion in the last year while the United Arab Emirate approved largest defense budget in its history.

Overall, it appears that geo-political tensions, increased AI’s integration demand, and concentration on equipping armies with anti-drone technologies, unmanned systems, missiles and fighter jets create significant profit-making opportunities for companies in defense industry. The recent financial results also signal at the robust demand trends. Therefore, it appears to be a great time to invest in defense-focused stocks and ETFs.

How Does SHLD is a Solid Invest Option?

There are multiple ways to invest in the defense industry. However, with the broader industry exposure, I believe exchange-traded-funds can increase investors potential to fully capitalize on the industry trends while lowering a risk factor associated with a single-stock investment. Global X Defense Tech ETF appears to be an attractive investment option because of its diversified portfolio of 40 stocks.

Prominent names, such as Palantir Technologies (PLTR), RTX Corp (RTX), Northrop Grumman (NOC), General Dynamics (GD) and Lockheed Martin (LMT) are SHLD’s top holdings. As its top 10 holdings make up 64% of entire portfolio, the fund’s performance is significantly dominated by its top stocks. Therefore, looking at these holdings can gives us a better view of the fund’s future performance and industry outlook.

Palantir Technologies, a software developer for the intelligence community, impressed investors with its AI exposure and robust demand for its products. Its shares soared 57% year to date, extending twelve months price gain to 373%. Its fourth-quarter revenue increased 36% year over year with expectations for a 31% growth in 2025. Palantir is the largest holding in SHLD portfolio, accounting for 13% total portfolio weight.

RTX, Northrop Grumman, General Dynamics and Lockheed Martin ended 2024 with the record backlogs. RTX’s 2024 revenue of $80.8 billion increased 9% year over year while adjusted earnings per share of $5.73 increased 13%. The company expects to extend the revenue and earning growth trend in 2025, supported by a record backlog of $273 billion. It anticipates 4% to 6% organic revenue growth in 2025 with double-digit earnings growth.

Northrop Grumman expects mid-single-digit revenue and a high mid-single-digit earnings growth in 2024 with expectations to return 100% of its $3 billion in free cash flows. Whereas, General Dynamics reported year over year earnings growth of 14.1% in 2024. With more than $90 billion in backlog, the company appears optimistic about its 2025 outlook. It expects revenue growth of around 5.5% in 2024 and EPS in the range of $14.80 compared to $13.63 per share in the previous year.

Shares of Leonardo (DRS) and Rheinmetall AG (OTCPK:RNMBF) soared sharply in the last twelve months as their revenue, profitability and backlogs continue to mark new highs quarter over quarter. Rheinmetall’s revenue increased by 36% and earnings per share surged by more than 70% year over year in the first nine months of 2024. In the earnings call, its CEO Armin Papperger claimed that the company is experiencing growth like it has never seen before. Here is what he stated:

Rheinmetall is needed, as demonstrated by our numerous successful orders. We are experiencing growth like we have never seen before in the Group. We have entered into pioneering collaborations and have promising projects in many countries – for example in the USA, Great Britain, Italy and Ukraine. We have major orders in our pipeline, which will ensure further growth in sales in the years to come. Overall, the defense industry continued to experience unprecedented demand due to tense geo-political conditions. Corporations are likely to extended their robust financial growth momentum in 2025, which will fuel their share and dividend returns.

The fund’s recent price performance also demonstrates investors’ increasing confidence in the defense sector. SHLD’s share price rallied 34% in 2024 while year to date gain of 9% signals a potential acceleration in price returns in 2025. The financial metrics of defense companies also indicate that the uptrend is fully supported by a blistering growth. The dividend returns are also expected to increase in 2025 given a record-breaking profitability.

Besides the portfolio structure and share price momentum, SHLD’s other key characteristics also appear attractive. For instance, despite a significant price outperformance, the fund is trading around 22.7x earnings, up slightly from the S&P 500’s 21.22x. Its price to book ratio is also in-line with the broader market index. Whereas, the risk indicators, such as beta and standard deviation, make it a less volatile investment option than the other major indices. With $808 million in assets under management and 384K in average daily share trading volume, the fund has enough liquidity to be considered for both short and long-term investment. Its expense ratio of 0.50% is in-line with the median of all ETFs.

In Conclusion

With the prospects for continued growth in defense spending and a race to achieve strategic advantage, it is a great time to invest in the defense industry. Investment options like Global X Defense Tech ETF can enable investors to capitalize on the broader industry trends while lowering a risk factor. The ETF also earned a buy rating based on Seeking Alpha quant rating due to its robust share price momentum, low-risk and healthy liquidity. With attractive valuations and strong earnings growth power, the ETF also offers a solid entry point for new investors.

data.title: Hertz: Another Chance To Sell
data.description:
Over the last couple of years, one of the worst performing stocks has been Hertz (NASDAQ:HTZ). The rental car giant has seen its shares plunge as a badly timed investment in electric vehicles has led to large losses and mounting debt. Recently, shares have rallied quite a bit from their 52-week low, but this remains a name that's in a very tough spot financially.

Previous coverage of the name:

It was nearly a year ago when I last took a look at this company, continuing to believe that it was in a tough spot. Hertz was looking to dump a lot of electric vehicles, which resulted in huge depreciation expenses and lower than expected residual sales values. The rise in US interest rates was also troubling for a company that relied significantly on debt to finance its fleet.

Since that article in March 2024, Hertz shares have lost more than a third of their value. That's a tremendous drop when you consider the S&P 500 is up about 18% over the same time. The plunge could have been even worse, but shares have rallied from their multi-year low of $2.47 to over $4.50 a share at one point on Tuesday. Overall, since I went to a sell rating on the name back in December 2022, shares have lost about 73% of their value.

Last week's report:

A little bit before the long holiday weekend, the company reported its fourth quarter results. The headline numbers were not good, as revenues of $2.04 billion were down 7% year over year and missed street estimates by $90 million. The company reported a non-GAAP loss of $1.18, missing the average estimate by almost 50 cents a share. This was the third straight quarter where the company missed estimates on both the top and bottom lines, and the sixth straight bottom line miss.

The Q4 2024 revenue decline came from two main factors. First, average rentable vehicle was down about 6% over the prior year period, mostly due to the company's de-fleeting of 30,000 electric vehicles. Second, revenues per day were down more than 2%. Currently, street analysts see limited growth in the top line this year, but not enough to get back to 2023 levels. Hertz will be looking to generate more revenues per day from its vehicles, while operating a smaller fleet than it previously had.

The problem for Hertz is that expenses are too great currently. Despite the revenue decrease, direct vehicle operating expenses rose over the prior year period. Even though vehicle depreciation improved a bit, the company also saw interest expenses shoot up thanks to the growing debt pile. As a result, the $479 million GAAP loss in the period was up significantly from the $348 million reported a year earlier.

Balance sheet is not in good shape:

Hertz finished 2024 with a little less than $600 million in non-restricted cash on the balance sheet, down about $175 million for the year. However, total debt rose by more than $640 million to over $16.3 billion. The company produced operating cash flow of over $2.2 billion in 2024, down $250 million from a year earlier. Unfortunately, it also spent more than $2.8 billion more on vehicles in 2024 over total sales proceeds. As a reminder, Hertz over the long term is buying new vehicles but then selling used ones at lower prices, so unless it continues to take the fleet down in large numbers, there's not a ton of room for major free cash flow here.

With the rise in interest rates in recent years, Hertz is in a tough spot. As the 10-K filing shows on page 113, over $9 billion of the company's debt comes due by the end of next year, with almost all of the rest due by the end of 2029. A number of these borrowings, including vehicle-related ones, came earlier in this decade and featured very low rates. Last year, interest expenses were about 10.6% of revenues, up from less than 8.5% the year prior, and all of these re-financings that are needed won't help that scenario.

The evolving rental car industry:

Hertz finds itself in a very interesting place currently. Travelers and some business customers still have a need for vehicle rentals, even if only for a short time period. The pandemic obviously crushed demand, not only from reduced consumer travels, but the increase in working from home. Hertz found out the hard way that electric vehicles were not in demand from many renters, and those users unfamiliar with the new technology led to increased repair costs and higher than expected depreciation.

In 2025, however, the biggest threat may not be purely an economic one. Electric vehicle giant Tesla (TSLA) is set to launch its robo-taxi business in June in Austin, Texas, with multiple other cities later in the year and rolling out even more over time. Other competitors like Google's (GOOG) (GOOGL) Waymo are already providing hundreds of thousands of paid rides per month. As more options like ride-sharing become available, potentially pushing prices of those services way down, the need for vehicle rentals may take a hit.

A look at the current valuation:

With Hertz losing money currently and expected to post only a very small profit next year, the stock looks expensive on a price to earnings basis. Given Monday's rally, shares go for about 48 times next year's currently expected earnings, a tremendous premium to the less than 7 times that competitor Avis Budget Group (CAR) goes for. Avis is in a much better place financially, producing a profit last year (after restructuring costs), while not burning as much cash as Hertz did.

On the analyst side of things, the bears are certainly in charge. The current average price target is $3.39, implying about 25% downside from Tuesday's high. While being very positive on Hertz over time, it seems that the street has finally realized how bad the situation is here. A year ago, the average price target was nearly $10 a share, and less than three years ago it was pushing $30 at its peak.

Final thoughts and recommendation:

After reporting downbeat results for Q4 last week, Hertz continues to be in a tough spot financially. The company's decision to reduce its fleet size led to a drop in revenues, but costs are not coming down at a similar pace. With a bad bet on electric vehicles still working its way through the system, cash burn has certainly continued, increasing overall debt levels at a time where Hertz has a mountain of borrowings to refinance.

With a troubled balance sheet and an uncertain industry moving forward, I'm going to keep a sell rating on this stock. It will take a lot of effort to get this company back to positive overall cash flow, and that might result in a further hit to revenues. The company will have some tough re-financings ahead of it, and it really could use to raise a large amount of money through equity, but it has a less than $1.5 billion market cap. With a high valuation currently, it would not surprise me if recent gains don't last.

data.title: Silicon Valley Conquers The Pentagon As Defense Tech Explodes
data.description:
For a few months now, I’ve been referring to today’s heightened geopolitical climate as the “new red Cold War,” where artificial intelligence [AI]—not necessarily fighter jets and nuclear weapons—serves as the primary battleground between the U.S. and its adversaries, most notably Russia and China.

The numbers tell a compelling story. According to one research firm, the global AI market in aerospace and defense is projected to surge from approximately $28 billion today to a staggering $65 billion by 2034. That’s a solid 9.91% compound annual growth rate [CAGR]. North America alone represents $10.43 billion of this market, and it’s growing even faster at 10.02% annually.

Palantir’s Meteoric Rise

We’re seeing a dramatic reshuffling of the traditional defense sector hierarchy.

Take Palantir Technologies (PLTR). The AI-focused company—founded in 2003 by Peter Thiel, among others, and named for a magical artifact from Lord of the Rings—has seen its stock soar approximately 61% so far this year, after returning a massive 340% in 2024.

Meanwhile, traditional defense giants like Lockheed Martin (LMT), Northrop Grumman (NOC) and General Dynamics (GD) have been struggling, with their combined market value now lower than that of Palantir, whose chief technology officer, Shyam Sankar, recently called the competition between the U.S. and China an “AI arms race.”

And let’s not discount the influence of Elon Musk’s cost-cutting Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) initiative. We could see a push to modernize military procurement, prioritizing software, drones, and robots over traditional hardware. DOGE has already sent tremors through the defense sector, with traditional contractor stocks taking hits as new players like SpaceX, OpenAI and Anduril Industries gain ground.

Vice President Vance Pushes for AI in Paris

This shift isn’t happening in a vacuum, of course. The Trump administration, with Vice President JD Vance at the forefront, is actively pushing for AI development while taking a notably different approach from our European allies.

At the AI Action Summit in Paris last week, Vance made it clear that the U.S. won’t let excessive regulation stifle innovation in this critical sector. “We need our European friends in particular to look to this new frontier with optimism rather than trepidation,” Vance told the audience, which included world leaders, CEOs and scientists from over 100 countries.

This pro-growth stance has already benefited U.S. chipmakers like Intel, whose shares ended last week up more than 23% on news of increased support for domestic chip production. The company has its work cut out for it: Today, some 90% of the world’s most advanced chips are made by the Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC).

Even Google, once hesitant about military contracts, has reversed its stance on AI weapons development. This shift signals a broader change in Silicon Valley’s relationship with defense contracts, opening up new investment opportunities in tech companies that previously stayed away from the sector.

Europe’s Defense Tech Boom Is Just Getting Started

The transformation isn’t limited to the U.S. Facing its own security challenges with Russia, Europe is seeing unprecedented growth in defense tech investment. Venture capital [VC] funding in defense technology hit a record $5.2 billion in 2024, according to a new report by Dealroom data. This marks an incredible fivefold jump from six years ago, making defense one of the fastest growing VC sectors in Europe right now.

For investors, this creates what I see as a once-in-a-generation opportunity. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s target of 3% of GDP for defense spending—roughly $1 trillion annually—suggests sustained government investment in the sector, despite Musk’s promise to cut costs. The key question isn’t whether there will be spending, but rather which companies will capture it.

Positioning for the Biggest Defense Tech Shift in Decades

So where should investors be looking? I see three key areas:

First, companies at the intersection of AI and defense, like Palantir, that are already proving their worth in military applications. These firms are positioning themselves as essential partners in modern warfare capabilities.

Second, domestic semiconductor manufacturers like Intel (INTC) that are critical to both AI development and national security. The Trump administration’s focus on U.S. production could provide significant tailwinds for these companies.

And third, emerging defense tech companies that are disrupting traditional military procurement. While many are not publicly traded yet—Anduril being one such example—keeping an eye on this space could reveal early opportunities as they come to market.

We’re witnessing the biggest transformation in defense technology since the advent of nuclear weapons, and I believe that those who position themselves in this new “AI arms race” could see substantial returns as this multi-decade trend unfolds.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
